data.title: HSBC Holdings Continues To Reward Shareholders
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The largest position in our portfolio is HSBC Holdings plc (NYSE:HSBC), due to its share price going up by 39% over the last 12 months.

In August last year, we shared our view on their FH 2024 financial results. We continued a string of Buy stances. One would have to return to 2019 to find our neutral stance with a hold stance.

Towards the end of the pandemic, we shared with the SA community that it was time to buy, as we stated that HSBC was "extremely cheap". The price was $21.39 per ADR and has since returned 240%. It even surpassed the great-performing S&P 500 which delivered 82.6%.

But we cannot rest on our laurels. The past is the past. We are investing for the future.

With the ADR now standing at $56.88 is it time to sell, hold, or even buy at this level?

Let us look under the hood.

HSBC FY 2024 Financial Results

Net profit for FY 2024 was $25 billion, which was an improvement of $440 million, or 1.8%. This works out to an EPS of $1.24 per share. The ADR consists of 5 ordinary shares. As such, the earnings per ADR was $6.2. With a share price of $56.96, we get a P/E of 9.2

In the context of the S&P 500 P/E being 27.55, HSBC is still attractively priced. A more relevant comparison would be with JPMorgan Chase (JPM) which has a P/E of 15.3

Another way to look at valuation is the price to Tangible Net Book Value. Here, we will see that HSBC has been able to increase the NTBV over the last couple of years. As shareholders, we like that too.

JPM’s P/B is as high as 2.41 on a TTM basis.

As we usually remind our readers, the two most important sources of income for banks are net interest income and fees.

HSBC’s NII decreased by as much as $3.1 billion to $43.8 billion last year. That is a drop of 6.6%

To mitigate reductions in NII, HSBC has hedged large parts of the sensitivity towards the NII. As of the end of 2024, this hedge had a balance of $529 billion.

We have to assume that the net interest margin peaked in 2023.

The Return on Tangible Equity, or ROTE, remains good at 14.6%. It was the same as in 2023.

After the GFC of 2008, all banks are holding more equity. HSBC’s Common Equity Tier-1 ratio is stable at 14.9%

We always like to monitor their Estimated Credit Losses. In this latest FY, the ECL came in at $3.4 billion.

$1.8 billion came from their Commercial Banking division and $0.2 billion came from Global Banking and Market. This included $0.4 billion as stage 3 charges relating to the commercial real estate sector in mainland China and $0.1 billion from the Hong Kong real estate sector.

A charge of $ 0.1 billion came from one single CMB customer in the UK. The ECL from the division of Wealth and Private Banking was quite large at $1.3 billion.

It is important to look at ECL in conjunction with the size of their total loan book. The ECLs are only 36 bps of the average gross loans. That was an increase of 4 bps from FY 2023.

Returning capital to shareholders

This is important to investors. Not just for us, but also for one of their largest shareholders, Ping An Insurance of P.R.C. In 2022 they became very vocal about HSBC's low payout ratio. They also wanted the bank to separate the Asian business from the rest in two separate companies. That they did not succeed with.

But, a more shareholder-friendly return of capital to shareholders did commence.

That is also one of the reasons we have seen a revival in the share price over the last couple of years.

The dividend for FY 2024 is $0.87 per share, or $4.35 per ADR.

On a TTM basis, we get a dividend yield of 7.6%. That is more than 4 x JPM’s 1.7% dividend yield. For foreign investors, like ourselves, we do not pay tax on dividends, and since it is the same in Hong Kong, where we own the HSBC shares, we get dividends without any withholding tax. If we were to own JPM in the U.S., we would have to pay a 30% withholding tax before receiving dividends in our bank account. The difference is huge.

On top of the dividend, HSBC bought back 9% of their shares in 2024 and commenced another buyback program to buy back up to $2 billion of their shares within the first quarter of this year.

$47.7 billion is a large amount of money. If we calculate what that means on an average yearly basis we “received” $1.33 per share.

That is in effect a yield of 12% per year.

Risk to Thesis

One risk is an increase in ECLs. This could come with an increase in property mortgages ending up in their Stage 3 bucket. However, we do not think that this is very likely.

For mortgages in their ring-fenced UK market, the average LTV was 53%, and for Hong Kong, it was 63% at the end of 2024. This is somewhat higher than the 50% and 57% LTV recorded in their 2022 annual report. It is still giving the bank good buffers for further drops in real estate prices.

Other risks, which all banks have to deal with are from their trading activities.

We as investors face the risk of a lower share price for HSBC and a reduction in their dividends.

Conclusion

After having gone through most of their 460-page annual report, based on our limited understanding and our belief in good faith, we still think that HSBC is good value at the present share price.

We do not want to give a price target, but it would still give a yield of 5.6% and have a P/E of a reasonable 12.3 if the share price for the ordinary shares trading in Hong Kong was HKD 120. That is 50% higher than what it is now.

Therefore, we believe that there is still upside potential from here on.

Our buy stance remains.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 02/22/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: PVAL: A Low Valuation Fund Outperforming Peers
data.description:
The Putnam Focused Large Cap Value ETF (NYSEARCA:PVAL) is an actively managed fund with a strong value profile and a selective approach to building a portfolio that has been delivering solid performance compared to other value funds.

While the current high valuations seen in the overall market strengthen the case for value-oriented investment strategies, it is interesting to watch whether PVAL can continue to outperform, despite its low exposure to technology, a sector that has led the bull market in recent years.

ETF Description & Highlights

PVAL is an ETF that provides exposure primarily to U.S. large-capitalization companies with value characteristics. It is an actively managed fund that selects companies with market capitalization similar to the Russell 1000 Value index, which is a subset of the parent Russell 1000 index, a typical benchmark for large- and mid-cap stocks.

PVAL’s portfolio consists of companies that the fund’s manager believes are undervalued in the market, taking into consideration aspects such as valuation metrics, financial strength, growth prospects, competitive position in the respective industry, future earnings forecast, cash flows, and dividends.

As of January 31, 2025, PVAL had $2.1 billion in assets under management, invested in a portfolio of 44 companies, with an average market cap of $115.1 billion. The allocation is skewed toward large caps, as expected, with 41.6% of total assets, while the allocation to mid caps is also representative, accounting for 34.4% of the fund, followed by mega caps at 20.0% and a marginal exposure to small caps, which make up 1.3%. In contrast, the Russell 1000 index, represented by the iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB), has a significantly higher allocation to mega and large caps, with 42.9% and 32.8%, respectively, reflecting a market cap-weighted approach that emphasizes larger companies.

PVAL's top ten holdings are Citigroup (4.51%), Walmart (4.03%), Bank of America (3.92%), Exxon Mobil (3.47%), Capital One (3.45%), Thermo Fisher Scientific (3.36%), Oracle (3.25%), UnitedHealth Group (2.97%), Hilton Worldwide (2.89%), and AIG (2.74%), which together represent 35% of total assets. The fund’s top holdings are weighted toward value-driven sectors like financial services (Citigroup, Bank of America, Capital One, and AIG) and healthcare (Thermo Fisher Scientific, UnitedHealth Group), which alongside holdings in consumer staples (Walmart) and energy (Exxon Mobil), reinforce the fund’s core investment strategy.

The following table includes a list of large-cap funds focused on the value-oriented strategies that will serve as the benchmark for PVAL in this analysis. The first one is the Vanguard Value ETF (VTV), the largest fund in the group, managing $135 billion and employing a multi-factor approach. Next is the iShares Russell 1000 Value ETF (IWD), derived from the Russell 1000 index, which is also a large fund with $64 billion in assets under management and a significant allocation to mid-cap stocks. The iShares S&P 500 Value ETF (IVE) utilizes three fundamental factors to select value equities from the S&P 500 index. Another key player, the iShares Core S&P US Value ETF (IUSV), is a subset of the S&P 900 index, while the Schwab US Large-Cap Value ETF (SCHV) follows a market cap-weighted approach with a strong bias toward mid caps. Lastly, the Vanguard Mega Cap Value ETF (MGV) specifically targets mega-cap stocks, with a relatively concentrated portfolio of 137 holdings.

From a sector allocation perspective, PVAL's largest allocation is to the financial services sector, with 22.1% of total equities, followed by healthcare at 15.4%, consumer staples at 11.7%, industrials at 11.5%, technology at 10.0%, consumer discretionary at 8.2%, energy at 7.1%, utilities at 5.2%, basic materials at 4.5%, communication services at 2.7%, and real estate at 1.7%. Relative to the Russell 1000 index, PVAL is overweight in financial services (+8.2%), consumer staples (+6.3%), and healthcare (+5.3%), but underweight in technology (-21.7%), communication services (-7.0%), and consumer discretionary (-3.1%).

The fund has a selective allocation to financial services, its largest sector, but with holdings across different industries, such as global banks Citigroup and Bank of America, investment banking Goldman Sachs, asset manager Apollo Global, credit provider Capital One Financial, and insurer AIG.

Meanwhile, PVAL’s holdings in consumer staples, PVAL’s second-largest sector, focus on three core industries: hypermarkets (Walmart and BJ’s Wholesale Club Holdings), soft drinks (Coca-Cola), and tobacco (Philip Morris International), while the fund’s allocation to industrials, another core sector for PVAL, includes companies from different segments, such as defense and aerospace (Northrop Grumman), logistic (FedEx), low-cost airline ( Southwest), and equipment manufacturer (Ingersoll Rand).

When compared to the peer group, PVAL is slightly overweight in consumer staples (+2.8%), basic materials (+1.7%), and financial services (+1;0%), as it has the largest exposure to consumer staples and basic materials among all peers. On the other hand, PVAL is underweight in technology (-6.0%), real estate (-1.6%), and communication services (-1.1%), as funds IVE and IUSV maintain a somewhat aggressive allocation to technology for a value fund at the 24% level.

Low Valuations

PVAL's average price-to-earnings ratio (P/E) of 15.6x represents a roughly 30% discount to the Russell 1000 index, with about half of this valuation gap due to PVAL’s underweight exposure to high-valuation sectors like technology and consumer discretionary, while PVAL’s stock selection also plays an important role here. This is evidenced by the low valuation of some of the fund’s core holdings in sectors such as consumer discretionary (PulteGroup: P/E 8.3x, General Motors: P/E 5.3x), technology (Oracle: P/E 27.4x, Qualcomm: P/E 14.1x), and healthcare (UnitedHealth: P/E 18.5x, McKesson: P/E 17.7x).

The fund’s earnings growth profile is lower than the Russell 1000 index, driven by its low exposure to high-growth sectors such as technology, alongside modest growth in some of the fund’s top holdings, such as Citigroup (EPS Fwd: 1.3%), Capital One (EPS Fwd: -4.1%), and Thermo Fisher Scientific (EPS Fwd: 0.3%),

On the profitability side, PVAL’s average EBITDA margin of 26.5% is nearly six percentage points lower than the Russell 1000 index, reflecting its low exposure to big tech names, while core holdings such as Walmart and McKesson are typically low-margin businesses with EBITDA margins below 10%.

PVAL’s P/E ratio is the lowest among peers, with an average 10% discount to the peer group. This valuation gap is largely driven by PVAL’s low exposure to technology, especially relative to funds IVE and ISUV, as their nearly 24% allocation to this sector is reflected in their P/E ratios in the range of 18%.

Outperforming The Peer Group

PVAL total returns over the past three years have been slightly higher than the Russell 1000 index, as the strong outperformance in the bearish year of 2022 more than offset weaker performance in the following two years, as broader equity indexes delivered solid gains on the heels of a strong bull market. This pattern is largely expected, as value-oriented funds like PVAL tend to outperform during negative years but have struggled to keep pace with the overall market in a more bullish environment, given the recurrent outperformance of growth stocks over the past years. Compared to the peer group, PVAL has outpaced all funds in this peer group year after year since its inception in May 2021, with the exception of 2022, when it ranked third, as MGV and VTV were more resilient that year, showing smaller losses during that period.

PVAL’s volatility has been lower than the Russell 1000 index, as expected for a value fund, while its beta of 0.91 was in line with most peers, just above MGV, VTV, and SCHV.

PVAL’s average spread of 0.04% is arguably low, driven by a reasonable average trading volume of $10 million, while other value funds have shown spreads as low as 0.01% and 0.02%, thanks to their heavy volume in general. On the other hand, its turnover of 54% is the highest in the peer group, leading to an expense ratio of 0.56%, which is much higher than peers, likely due to the fact that it is an actively managed fund. PVAL’s dividend yield of 1.28% is the lowest in the peer group, reflecting its low exposure to real estate and its stock picking in healthcare and consumer staples. On the other hand, PVAL’s tracking error of around 7% is in line with peers, while its adjusted returns, as measured by its Sharpe ratio of 0.61 for the three-year period is the highest in the group, driven by strong returns and volatility in line with peers.

Final Thoughts

PVAL’s investment approach favoring undervalued companies is reflected in a low price-to-earnings ratio of 15.6x, which is a positive for the fund, given the currently stretched valuations in the overall market, as it is likely to limit downside risk in the event of a stock market correction.

In addition, with a concentrated portfolio of 44 holdings compared to peers, the fund’s active management philosophy is expected to have played a central role in its stock selection, as evidenced by a high turnover of 54% for a value fund. Nevertheless, it is fair to say that its investment style appears to have added value to the fund over this relatively short time since its inception in May 2021, given its outperformance compared to other value funds over this period. This happened despite PVAL’s underweight allocation to the technology sector, which has been one of the biggest winners over the past few years.

In summary, while investors should bear in mind that relatively concentrated portfolios like PVAL may be subject to periods of underperformance, due to potential lower correlation to broader equity indexes, my take on PVAL is overall positive, and I view it as one of the good funds to hold for the value-oriented portion of an investor’s portfolio.

data.title: Cadence Design Systems: Still Find It Hard To Justify The Premium Valuation Multiple
data.description:
Investment action

I recommended a hold rating for Cadence Design Systems, Inc. (NASDAQ:CDNS) when I wrote about it in late 2023 because the stock valuation was too high. Based on my current outlook and analysis, I recommend a hold rating. I still have the view that CDNS is well-positioned for long-term growth. However, I still find it hard to justify the valuation premium vs. peers, especially with growth expected to decelerate.

Review

CDNS reported 4Q24 revenue of $1.36 billion, up 27% y/y. Bookings performance deserves a highlight as CDNS booked >$2.5 billion in new orders, driving total backlog to a record $6.8 billion. The book-to-bill ratio also remained above 1, indicating a healthy underlying demand trend and a strong revenue pipeline for FY25. Strong growth was coupled with strong adj EBIT margin expansion from ~43% in 4Q23 to 46% in 4Q24. Collectively, this drove a solid adj EPS performance, coming in at $1.88 vs. $1.38 in 4Q23. Just by looking at this, you would imagine FY25 to be another strong year. But, no. Despite these positives, management gave a soft guidance for FY25, calling for revenue in the range between $5.14 billion and $5.22 billion (implying growth decelerating to 11.6% y/y at the midpoint). The way CDNS’s share price reacted post the results reinforced my view that the current high valuation remains uninvestable – any miss in expectation could send the stock price into a nosedive.

That said, I must say that CDNS fundamentals remain attractive. In my opinion, CDNS's long-term outlook likely remains intact, given its exposure to AI and cloud computing verticals, both of which are experiencing significant growth. The idea is that as the world demands more advanced AI applications, it will drive up the complexity of semiconductor chips, and this ultimately drives up the demand for chip design automation (to ensure optimal productivity and efficiency). This is a strong tailwind for CDNS, and the adoption of its Cerebrus tool (CDNS’s AI-driven chip design tool) has now been used in >750 tape-outs, with >300 in 4Q24 alone. This indicates that semiconductor firms are increasingly looking to optimize designs for AI workloads. We can also infer how strong the demand is from CDNS IP business, which saw a 28% y/y increase in 4Q24. The surge in AI compute demand is also driving growth in CDNS hardware business. In 4Q24, CDNS’s Palladium Z3 and Protium X3 platforms saw their strongest quarter ever, with hardware revenue up 61% sequentially and 150% y/y, as these platforms help clients address the need for AI chip verification. Viewed together simply, as long as the world demands more AI and continues to advance AI technology (which I strongly agree it will continue), CDNS will continue to grow.

The problem is how fast CDNS can grow. Having a positive growth outlook and being able to grow rapidly are two different things, and it is particularly important when the stock trades at premium multiple to peers with similar expected growth rates. In the case of CDNS, management guidance points to decelerating growth despite what I thought to be a strong secular tailwind that CDNS is capitalizing on. Specifically, management expects core EDA (electronic design automation) business revenue to grow in the high single digits y/y, down from the mid-teens range seen in FY24. It was also noted that 4Q24 included a large volume of software renewals, which means demand has been pulled forward, and this could potentially weigh on near-term growth rates—another factor that could weigh on investor sentiment, particularly given Cadence’s premium valuation.

Furthermore, CDNS growth is also at risk due to its exposure to China—revenue growth decelerated to 13% y/y in FY24. While design activity remains strong in China, the geopolitical risk cannot be ignored, especially with regards to export controls. While management remains optimistic about long-term demand, I think the near-term demand outlook is up to anyone’s guess, and this is not a favorable thing for CDNS stock sentiment.

Valuation

CDNS continues to trade at a steep premium to its two primary peers: Synopsys (SNPS) and ANSYS (ANSS). Let me give you some numbers for comparison. SNPS is expected to grow 13% for the next two years and ANSS to grow at 9%. Viewed together, the growth outlook for these two peers ranges between high single digits to mid-teens percentage. Because of the higher growth, SNPS trades at a modest premium vs. ANSS at ~28x vs. ~27x NTM PE. CDNS, on the other hand, has FY25 guidance calling for low-teens growth. If we simply compare it against peers, I think it's fair to assume CDNS should trade at ~27.5x. This is not the case. CDNS trades at ~40x NTM PE. I still struggle to convince myself this premium is warranted. It is not as if peers are not exposed to the same long-term secular trend. As such, I remained with the view that CDNS is still very expensive. The growth outlook does not justify such a steep premium, and I believe the market is gradually recognizing my view as CDNS NTM PE has seen multiple periods of weakness since late 2023. If CDNS growth does not accelerate, I expect valuation multiples to continue de-rating to where peers trade.

Final thoughts

My recommendation remains hold. While the long-term demand is likely intact, CDNS's growth outlook for FY25, as per guidance, is weak. The stock’s premium valuation relative to peers like SNPS and ANSS is also concerning, especially given management’s cautious outlook. If growth fails to re-accelerate, I expect further valuation multiple compression. That said, I would say that CDNS remains a high-quality company, and should growth pick up, the stock premium multiple could see support.

data.title: CarGurus: Momentum Breaking On Revenue Deceleration, Wait For A Better Price
data.description:
So far in the Q4 earnings season, even slight earnings misses have decimated the rallies of momentum stocks. CarGurus (NASDAQ:CARG), the largest online marketplace for used cars, had been enjoying a massive rebound rally that at one point nearly doubled its stock relative to the past year.

The company just reported Q4 earnings results, and despite strong paid net adds to its dealership base, the company fell below consensus marks on revenue for both the current quarter as well as its outlook, leading the stock down ~10%. In my view, it's the start of a correction that will bring CarGurus back to where it belongs in value-stock territory.

I last wrote a neutral opinion on CarGurus in November, when the stock was trading closer to $35 per share. Since then, CarGurus' Q4 results have broken its rally, which I think is on track to bring the stock back down to earth. CarGurus is no longer exactly a growth stock: its overall revenue growth is expected to cling to the mid single digits, and despite meaningful advances on profitability margins, the company shouldn't trade at a stark premium to the rest of the market. I'm maintaining a watch-and-wait approach on this stock, and I am reiterating my neutral opinion.

To me, the biggest risk ahead for CarGurus is the very likely possibility that used car prices increase. Trump's planned tariffs on Mexico are still on the negotiating table: but if they pass, the general consensus is that the prices of auto parts will rise sharply in the U.S., bringing up the prices of new vehicles as well as the parts to replace older vehicles.

U.S. consumers are already inflation-strapped. CarGurus has enjoyed a rebound in dealer activity and car sales since the early days of the pandemic, but continuous rising prices (as well as persistently high auto financing rates) may put a stopper on demand.

That being said, there are still positives that we should be aware of: CarGurus still maintains the distinction of being the highest-traffic site in the used car marketplace space. The industry is inherently cyclical, and as such, if and when sales rebound, CarGurus would be in a strong position to benefit. In addition, we note the company's dealer expansion overseas as a good signal, as international expansion represents tremendous potential for CarGurus' overall TAM.

All in all, however, I view the bull/bear case for CarGurus to be rather balanced. Steer clear for now and wait for a better price before buying back in.

Q4 download

Let's now go through CarGurus' latest quarterly results in greater detail. To me, the fourth-quarter print was a mixed quarter, delivering disappointing news on the top line while the company managed to continue ramping up its adjusted EBITDA. Take a look at the earnings summary below:

CarGurus' revenue grew only 2% y/y to $228.5 million, decelerating three points from Q3's 5% y/y growth pace while also slightly missing Wall Street's expectations of $231.7 million (+4% y/y). The good news underneath the hood: the deceleration was primarily driven by the company's wholesale and product arms (each of which declined -55% y/y), while core marketplace revenue (which is what CarGurus earns from its paying dealerships in the form of subscription fees) increased 15% y/y, a growth rate in line with Q3.

At the same time, the company continued an aggressive expansion of paying dealers, both domestically and bbroad. The company added 326 paid dealers in the quarter, which was roughly the same as 332 dealers added in Q3 (and faster than only 177 dealers added in Q2).

Among the company's chief growth strategies is to boost its average revenue per paying dealership (QARSD, which grew 12% y/y in Q4), and its path to doing so is by releasing new features. For example, the company began offering price insights to participating dealerships in Q4, which it claimed helped dealers move through inventory faster. Per CEO Jason Trevisan's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

In Q4 alone, we recorded nearly 1 million price changes to the inventory we recommended a change on, up 33% from Q3, which drove faster turn times and significantly boosted dealers' ability, to maximize their profit. Additionally, we saw strong growth across our other Dealer Data Insights report.

Of dealers who leveraged our acquisition insights report, 69% of them acquired the vehicles we recommended, and 65% of the acquired vehicles sold within the estimated turn time. Our suite of data-driven, predictive analytics and actionable insights, presents a compelling value proposition for our dealers, and we will continue to release new insights across the dealers' workflows."

Meanwhile, on the profitability front, we note that CarGurus' adjusted EBITDA margins have kept climbing, hitting 33% in Q4: a 5-point improvement sequentially, and a 6-point improvement y/y.

Here, the company is demonstrating the wisdom of focusing back on its high-margin marketplace revenue. With marketplace revenue now over 90% of CarGurus' overall revenue, the company has hit 87% gross margins, which creates incredible room for operating leverage as CarGurus continues to scale.

Valuation and key takeaways

At best, however, I believe CarGurus to be only fairly valued. At current share prices near $33, CarGurus trades at a market cap of $3.49 billion. After we net off the $304.2 million of cash on CarGurus' latest balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $3.19 billion.

CarGurus only guides one quarter out at a time. Its Q1 outlook calls for $216-$236 million in revenue (5% growth at the midpoint), and 5 points of y/y adjusted EBITDA margin expansion to 28% (versus 23% in Q1'24).

Wall Street had been expecting ~9-10% growth for Q1 and for FY25, which is one of the biggest drivers behind CarGurus' post-earnings fall. If we carry CarGurus' implicit guidance of 5% y/y revenue growth and 5 points of y/y adjusted EBITDA margin expansion (implying 33% full-year margins) to the full year FY25, we arrive at $939.1 million in revenue and $309.9 million in adjusted EBITDA (+25% y/y).

This puts CarGurus' valuation multiples at:

  • 3.4x EV/FY25 revenue
  • 10.3x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA

In my view, for a company with macro risks hanging overhead, I'd prefer to wait for CarGurus to fall to an 8x FY25 adjusted EBITDA multiple, implying a price target of $27 and ~20% downside from current levels.

Keep an eye out for this stock, but don't rush in to buy just yet.

data.title: Ariel Investments Small/Mid Cap Value Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Markets worldwide defied expectations in 2024 led by the dominating performance of mega-cap technology stocks known as the “magnificent seven.1” Even though gains have been concentrated, the results of the U.S. election, strong earnings momentum and a robust labor market drove bullish sentiment. This ebullience extended beyond the equity market: gold delivered its best year since 2010, bitcoin more than doubled and the U.S. dollar neared a 20-year high. Meanwhile, uncertainty surrounding the new U.S. administration’s policies and the Federal Reserve signaling slower than anticipated easing in the year ahead drove small cap issues sharply lower in December. Although uncertainty and volatility are likely to remain elevated, the patient investor knows “stock prices trade on fundamentals. And when those solid fundamentals shine through, share prices rise. 2” Against this backdrop, the Ariel Small/Mid Cap Value Composite advanced +0.76% gross of fees (+0.65% net of fees) in the quarter, ahead of both the Russell 2500 Value Index and Russell 2500 Index, which returned -0.26% and +0.62%, respectively. Over the trailing one-year period, the Ariel Small/Mid Cap Value Composite increased +14.86% (+14.34% net of fees), significantly ahead of both the Russell 2500 Value Index and the Russell 2500 Index, which gained +10.98% and +12.00%, respectively.

Several stocks in the portfolio had strong returns in the quarter. Global cruise vacation company, Royal Caribbean Group (RCL), advanced on another quarterly earnings beat and subsequent raise in full-year guidance. Stronger than anticipated consumer demand, healthy onboard spend, robust pricing and solid cost containment boosted results.

Additionally, RCL is benefitting from several new megaships, more island destinations and a growing addressable market, including younger demographics who are “new-to-cruise.” The resiliency of the core cruise consumer, in combination with management’s superior operational expertise and revised earnings outlook, lays the foundation for RCL to sustain its powerful growth trajectory and strengthen its position as a leader in the global cruise industry.

Alternative asset manager, Carlyle Group Inc. (CG), also traded higher over the quarter with the U.S. election widely viewed as a positive catalyst across the industry. Investors expect the incoming administration to emphasize deregulation and exhibit a greater openness to business combinations compared to the prior regime. Hence, management’s positive commentary around the expectation for increased capital market activity, fee-related earnings growth, private equity fund appreciation as well as returning capital to shareholders via buybacks sent shares higher. At current levels, we believe CG trades at a healthy discount to our private market value estimate.

Additionally, shares of global leader in for-profit education, Adtalem Global Education (ATGE), jumped following another robust quarterly earnings beat driven by accelerating new student enrollment growth and a subsequent raise in fiscal year 2025 guidance. Revenue per student at Chamberlain and Walden also came in better than expected on tuition increases. Meanwhile, we remain encouraged by management’s solid execution of remediation initiatives at the medical and veterinary schools, where revenue growth outperformed and total enrollment trends are improving. As the number one grantor of nursing degrees in the U.S. and the largest producer of African American MDs, PhDs and nurses in the country, we remain confident ATGE will benefit from the healthcare worker shortage in the U.S.

Alternatively, shares of manufacturer and distributor of floorcovering products, Mohawk Industries Inc. (MHK) declined in the period as consumer demand and pricing remain under pressure due to secular headwinds in the housing market. Although quarterly earnings results were in-line with expectations, management lowered near-term guidance to account for losses resulting from recent U.S. hurricanes.

Meanwhile, management continues to successfully execute on productivity and cost restructuring initiatives, while also preparing the business for share gains as demand recovers. In our view, MHK’s healthy balance sheet and progress managing through economic cycles should help the company benefit from long-term growth in residential remodeling, new home construction and commercial projects.

Madison Square Garden Entertainment Corp. (MSGE) was another underperformer in the quarter. Despite the delivery of strong earnings results, shares traded lower following a reduction to fiscal 2025 adjusted operating income guidance. The revision was driven by unique circumstances surrounding concert tour cancellations and higher costs associated with bringing sponsorship sales in-house.

Nonetheless, with marquee assets such as New York’s Madison Square Garden, Radio City Music Hall, Beacon Theatre and The Chicago Theater, we believe MSGE is well positioned to capitalize on strong demand for live entertainment. Additionally, new sales and renewal activity in the company’s hospitality business remains robust. MSGE recently announced multi-year sponsorship deals with Lenovo, its subsidiary Motorola Mobility, the Department of Culture and Tourism-Abu Dhabi, as well as a multi-year extension of its sponsorship deal with Verizon. In our view, MSGE’s portfolio generates stable cash flow that should enable further deleveraging. At current levels, the company is trading at a significant discount to our estimate of private market value.

Lastly, shares of live entertainment, media and technology company, Sphere Entertainment Co. (SPHR) traded lower over the period. We view the sell-off as an overreaction given investor’s overly-aggressive expectations for a quick MSG Networks debt resolution as well as a third Sphere franchise announcement. Looking ahead, we expect financial results will continue to ramp as the company scales its concert residencies, secures additional marquee sporting events and reaches higher venue utilization across show types.

Advertising, sponsorship and suite revenue should provide additional upside. Meanwhile, international expansion beyond Abu Dhabi remains a priority. In our view, the experiential immersive venue in Las Vegas and its franchise opportunities alongside the company’s two regional sports and entertainment networks present a long-term opportunity that remains meaningfully underappreciated at current trading levels.

We did not initiate or exit any positions in the quarter.

We believe the U.S. economy looks healthy and expect corporate profits to prove resilient. Although interest rates will likely settle at a higher structural level compared to the past decade and potential policies around tariffs and immigration present uncertainty, we think market friendly policies such as tax cuts and deregulation should support the broadening of the market beyond the Magnificent Seven. Once the performance gap between mega-cap stocks and smaller company counterparts narrows, our portfolios should be rewarded. We strongly believe the disciplined investor that stays the course and consistently owns differentiated businesses with solid competitive positioning and robust balance sheets will deliver superior returns over time.

1 The “Magnificent Seven” are the largest stocks in the S&P 500 Index driving market performance: Apple Inc. (AAPL), Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL), Meta Platforms Inc. (META), Microsoft Corp. (MSFT), NVIDIA Corp. (NVDA) and Tesla, Inc. (TSLA).

2 Hobson, Mellody and John W. Rogers Jr. “What the Stock market Taught Us This Year: Don’t Fall for These Investing Traps.” Wall Street Journal, 5 December 2023.

Investing in small-cap and mid-cap stocks is more risky and volatile than investing in large-cap stocks. The intrinsic value of the stocks in which the portfolio invests may never be recognized by the broader market. The portfolio is often concentrated in fewer sectors than its benchmarks, and its performance may suffer if these sectors underperform the overall stock market. Investing in equity stocks is risky and subject to the volatility of the markets.

Past performance does not guarantee future results. For the period ended 12/31/24 the performance (net of fees) for the Ariel Small/Mid Cap Value Composite for the 1-, 5-, and 10- year periods was +14.34%, +9.49%, and +8.30%, respectively. For the period ended 12/31/24 the performance for the Russell 2500 Value Index and the Russell 2500 Index for the 1-, 5-, and 10-year periods was +10.98%, +8.43%, and +7.80%, and +12.00%, +8.76%, and +8.85%, respectively. Ariel Composite Net of Fees returns are calculated by deducting: (1) for the period from inception to December 31, 2013, the maximum advisory fee in effect for the respective period, applied on a monthly basis; and (2) for the period from January 1, 2014 onwards, the actual monthly advisory fee (on an asset-weighted basis) accrued for the accounts in the composite, using the fee rates in place as of the most recent calendar quarter-end. Gross returns do not reflect the deduction of advisory fees. Client returns will be reduced by advisory fees and such other expenses as may be incurred in the management of the account. Advisory fees are described in Part 2 of Ariel’s Form ADV. Returns assume the reinvestment of dividends and other earnings. Returns are expressed in U.S. dollars. data.title: Cenovus Energy: Weak Quarter Is The Time To Buy
data.description:
Cenovus Energy (NYSE:CVE) reported a weak quarter. Left out of the conversation was a line in the report and conference call by management that the sale of some production was delayed. Such a comment is usually a sign by management that if they wait, they will get better prices. There was also the redemption of some preferred stock which decreases financial leverage for common stock investors. However, there was a concern about refining and another concern that debt climbed in a quarter that had negative free cash flow.

But quarterly variations in this very volatile industry are not all that uncommon. Furthermore, I have long held that the refineries were so badly managed by Husky that it would take some time to fix the situation. Management mentioned on the conference call what all was done in the latest fiscal year. But the market probably will not be satisfied until profits are where the market thinks they should be. Management, to their credit, indicated that there was more work to be done.

Refining

On the other hand, both the Toledo and Superior refineries ran all year while both improved operations. Superior was basically a major construction project that really needs a couple of years to run "and get the bugs out" that any major project has. A past article covered the Toledo fire and subsequent acquisition of the BP (BP) interest.

(Note: Cenovus Energy is a Canadian company that reports using Canadian dollars unless otherwise noted.)

The problem with something like this is that it gets solved first, and the benefits show up next. Notice that restart costs for both Toledo and Superior were mentioned. Restarting anything large is usually a messy process that costs serious money. That whole process has now ended. Next comes optimization for both of them.

The overall process is on the slow side because there are a lot of fixed assets. That generally means one thing gets fixed and management reviews the results before deciding on the next project.

Making things even harder for investors is the fluctuating spreads that are normal for refining. If the conference call was any indication, patience in the investment community is wearing thin. Management deserves a lot of credit for doing things right and resisting that "wearing thin".

Similar Situation

This problem is similar to the situation with Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) in that the CEO finally did a road presentation and just stated "it took two years" which was about two years longer than the market had patience for. The stock price paid the price. But now, finally, management is at the point where things are fixed, and the original strategy can be executed.

The big deal is that for all the talk about doing things right and supposedly rewarding that "right". The fact is that Mr. Market is very impatient. Doing things right is ok with the market as long as it is done in about two minutes. Now long-term, there is no doubt that the market rewards superior strategies. But the short-term pricing action can be a royal headache for management.

Growth

Another thing a weak quarter does is take the market concentration away from the long-term profitable growth story. Mr. Market often assumes that a weak performance will continue "forever" despite the progress made here to make sure that is not the case long-term.

Management emphasized the continuing growth projects so that the growth story shown above will continue. Now this company has long augmented organic growth with opportunistic acquisitions. That means the slow steady growth of a typically large company like this one will have an occasional growth spurt from an acquisition.

Similarly, both cash flow and free funds flow have grown even if the quarterly and annual results are on the erratic side due to commodity price volatility. Well run thermal producers like this one tend to cash flow on the generous side over time.

Earnings Summary

(Note: Cenovus Energy is a Canadian company that reports using Canadian dollars unless otherwise noted.)

Even though this is widely regarded as a weak quarter, cash flow was still on the generous side.

As shown above, this is a company with a growth capital budget as well as paying a dividend, repurchasing some preferred stock and (from time to time) buying back common stock as well. While debt did climb a little bit, the net debt figure is well within a reasonable range.

The stock is also likely punished because capital expenditures climbed at a time of weak pricing. Business schools teach that the safest time to expand is when it can be profitably done during a cyclical downturn or a time of industry weakness. Meanwhile, Mr. Market is looking for free cash flow and dividends "yesterday". He will likely be happy when the capital projects provide both the dividends and the free cash flow. He is not happy now.

Management can easily get the net debt figure to C$4 billion (which is the original target) again without a whole lot of stress on the cash flow. However, it would be hard to tell that from some of the conference call questions and other comments. For that reason, it may be best to get that net debt figure back to where it belongs to stop all the market worrying about it.

The biggest lesson for investors is that quarterly variations are the norm in this business. So, it is probably best to take advantage of the market attitude when a quarter like this gets reported.

Traders often make a good living by selling the good quarters and waiting for an inevitable quarter like this. Some have options strategies to enhance the income made from the stock price volatility.

But for long-term investors, the growth story remains intact even if the quarterly swings make for a bumpy ride.

Summary

Despite the record production and several solid growth projects on the table, the market focused upon the weak current quarterly report. But this industry has those reports interspaced with strong quarterly reports. The key is that there is nothing wrong with the company.

Finances are in good shape here which seriously reduces the main risk in this industry. More importantly, thermal operations often cash flow more than adequately even when profits are poor because a thermal project often has a large upfront cash requirement.

This management made a lot of acquisitions and shed a lot of partners in the process of gaining full control of its operations. Now it is going to take some time to straighten things out. But work is under way to do just that even if the gradual improvement shown is a bit slow for the market's patience.

The recent stock price weakness strengthens the strong buy case. Mr. Market will come around as the benefits of the growth projects and the margin improvement projects begin to show.

In the meantime, the base dividend was substantially increased in the current fiscal year. Management predicts more base dividend increases as all the profitable projects show results.

I would tend to hang onto this stock until the growth and profit improvement story changes. That is likely to be well into the future.

Risks

The biggest risk here is the loss of market patience with refining results because the pace of improving the situation is not to the liking of the market. This results in stock price action that can really give an investor a stomachache. It should work out in the long-run. But getting there is going to be bumpy.

Any upstream operation is subject to the volatility and low visibility of future commodity prices. A severe and sustained downturn could materially change the future outlook of the company.

To a certain extent the refining operations will protect against a widening of the discount to light oil benchmarks because the products made from refining are value added products that sell for the same amount regardless of the raw materials. This is probably the largest single benefit to the company at the current time.

Finances are investment grade which minimizing financial risks that smaller players face.

Management here has an above average track record in the long run. A loss of key personnel could prove to be a challenge to replace because of that record.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Ariel Investments Small Cap Value Concentrated Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Markets worldwide defied expectations in 2024 led by the dominating performance of mega-cap technology stocks known as the “magnificent seven.1” Even though gains have been concentrated, the results of the U.S. election, strong earnings momentum and a robust labor market drove bullish sentiment. This ebullience extended beyond the equity market: gold delivered its best year since 2010, bitcoin more than doubled and the U.S. dollar neared a 20-year high. Meanwhile, uncertainty surrounding the new U.S. administration’s policies and the Federal Reserve signaling slower than anticipated easing in the year ahead drove small cap issues sharply lower in December. Although uncertainty and volatility are likely to remain elevated, the patient investor knows “stock prices trade on fundamentals. And when those solid fundamentals shine through, share prices rise. 2” Against this backdrop, the Ariel Small Cap Value Concentrated Composite declined -0.54% gross of fees (-0.67% net of fees) in the quarter, outpacing the Russell 2000 Value Index’s -1.06% return, but shy of the +0.33% gain posted by the Russell 2000 Index. Over the trailing one-year period, the Ariel Small Cap Value Concentrated Composite increased +12.07% (+11.46% net of fees), outperforming the Russell 2000 Value Index’s +8.05% gain and the +11.54% return earned by the Russell 2000 Index.

Several stocks in the portfolio had strong returns in the quarter. Provider of wellness services onboard cruise ships and destination resorts, OneSpaWorld Holdings Ltd. (OSW), traded higher as strong consumer trends drove another significant earnings beat and subsequent raise in full year guidance. Meanwhile, management strengthened the balance sheet by paying down and restructuring its expensive first lien term loan as well as ramped up capital returns to shareholders through buybacks and dividends.

Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings Ltd. (NCLH) also advanced over the period following a top- and bottom-line earnings beat and subsequent raise in full-year guidance.

Stronger than anticipated consumer demand, healthy onboard spending, robust pricing, solid cost containment and continued progress on leverage reduction boosted results. Looking ahead, NCLH remains focused on right sizing its cost base and improving margins to further strengthen its foundation for sustainable and profitable growth. With a young average fleet and solid liquidity position, we remain enthusiastic about the name.

Additionally, supplier of residential thermal, comfort and security solutions, Resideo Technologies, Inc. (REZI) outperformed over the period. Despite a challenging macro environment, positive organic sales growth and structural cost efficiencies drove margin expansion. Encouraging commentary around improving consumer demand trends, new product offerings and cross-selling opportunities further aided share price appreciation. Also in the quarter, President and CEO, Jay Geldmacher, announced his intention to retire from his executive and board roles in 2025. Once a successor is identified, Mr. Geldmacher expects to become a senior advisor to the company to facilitate a smooth transition. In our view, this leadership change is indicative of the successful completion of the transformation plan the team initiated following the spin-out from Honeywell. We believe the company is entering a new phase of sustainable growth which will be driven by a secular preference for more connected smart home solutions and product innovation.

In contrast, logistics and cash management services provider, Brink’s Company (BCO) underperformed in the period. Despite the delivery of strong organic revenue growth and margin improvement across both the ATM managed services and digital retail solutions segments, currency headwinds and softness in its global services business drove an earnings miss. Although management lowered full year guidance, BCO continues to win margin accretive business, such as its recently announced long-term partnership with Sainsbury’s, one of the largest supermarkets in the United Kingdom.

Meanwhile, the company is making substantial investments in its technology platforms and returning capital to shareholders through dividends and share repurchases.

Shares of manufacturer and distributor of floorcovering products, Mohawk Industries Inc. (MHK) declined in the period as consumer demand and pricing remain under pressure due to secular headwinds in the housing market. Although quarterly earnings results were in-line with expectations, management lowered near-term guidance to account for losses resulting from recent U.S. hurricanes. Meanwhile, management continues to successfully execute on productivity and cost restructuring initiatives, while also preparing the business for share gains as demand recovers. In our view, MHK’s healthy balance sheet and progress managing through economic cycles should help the company benefit from long-term growth in residential remodeling, new home construction and commercial projects.

Lastly, U.S. direct-to-consumer pool and spa care services company, Leslie’s Inc. (LESL) traded lower following another consecutive quarter of disappointing financial and operating results. Soft consumer demand driven by weather-related headwinds and continued price sensitivity on large discretionary purchases weighed on the top-line. Product margins also remained under pressure as the company struggled to unwind its higher-cost inventory. In response, LESL’s new executive leadership team introduced three key strategic initiatives focused on personalizing communication and product offerings by leveraging local consumer information and data across different markets while improving existing-store sales and productivity. Although we have been deeply disappointed with this investment, we are optimistic the new efforts will drive meaningful improvements to the business and position the company for long-term success. At today’s valuation, LESL appears to have more upside than downside and the company’s loyal client base, vertically integrated supply chain, scale advantage and seamless customer experience remain differentiators.

We did not initiate or exit any positions in the quarter.

We believe the U.S. economy looks healthy and expect corporate profits to prove resilient. Although interest rates will likely settle at a higher structural level compared to the past decade and potential policies around tariffs and immigration present uncertainty, we think market friendly policies such as tax cuts and deregulation should support the broadening of the market beyond the Magnificent Seven. Once the performance gap between mega-cap stocks and smaller company counterparts narrows, our portfolios should be rewarded. We strongly believe the disciplined investor that stays the course and consistently owns differentiated businesses with solid competitive positioning and robust balance sheets will deliver superior returns over time.

Investing in small-cap companies is more risky and volatile than investing in large cap companies. A concentrated portfolio may be subject to greater volatility than a more diversified portfolio. The intrinsic value of the stocks in which the portfolio invests may never be recognized by the broader market. The portfolio is often concentrated in fewer sectors than its benchmarks, and its performance may suffer if these sectors underperform the overall stock market. Investing in equity stocks is risky and subject to the volatility of the markets.

Footnotes

1 The “Magnificent Seven” are the largest stocks in the S&P 500 Index driving market performance: Apple Inc. (AAPL), Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL), Meta Platforms Inc. (META), Microsoft Corp. (MSFT), NVIDIA Corp. (NVDA) and Tesla, Inc. (TSLA).

2 Hobson, Mellody and John W. Rogers Jr. “What the Stock market Taught Us This Year: Don’t Fall for These Investing Traps.” Wall Street Journal, 5 December 2023.

Past performance does not guarantee future results. For the one-year period ended 12/31/24 and since inception on April 30, 2020, the performance (net of fees) for the Ariel Small Cap Value Concentrated Composite was +11.46% and +14.88%, respectively. For the one-year period ended 12/31/24 and since inception on April 30, 2020 of the Ariel Small Cap Value Concentrated Composite, the performance for the Russell 2000 Value Index was +8.05% and +15.59%, respectively. And over the same periods the Russell 2000 Index delivered +11.54% and +13.56%, respectively. Ariel Composite Net of Fees returns are calculated by deducting the actual monthly advisory fee (on an asset-weighted basis) applicable to all accounts in the composite, using the fee rates in place as of the most recent calendar quarter-end. Gross returns do not reflect the deduction of advisory fees. Client returns will be reduced by advisory fees and such other expenses as may be incurred in the management of the account. Advisory fees are described in Part 2 of Ariel’s Form ADV. Returns assume the reinvestment of dividends and other earnings. Returns are expressed in U.S. dollars. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. The Ariel Small Cap Value Concentrated Composite differs from its primary benchmark in that it has dramatically fewer holdings and more concentration in fewer sectors.

The opinions expressed are current as of the date of this commentary but are subject to change. The information provided in this commentary does not provide information reasonably sufficient upon which to base an investment decision and should not be considered a recommendation to purchase or sell any particular security. There is no guarantee that any of the views expressed will come to fruition or any investment will perform as described.

As of 12/31/24, OneSpaWorld Holdings, Ltd. constituted 11.7% of the Ariel Small Cap Value Concentrated Composite (representative portfolio); Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings, Ltd. 8.4%; Resideo Technologies, Inc. 5.4%; Brink’s Company 8.5%; Mohawk Industries, Inc. 3.8%; and Leslie’s, Inc. 1.7%. Portfolio holdings are subject to change. The performance of any single portfolio holding is no indication of the performance of other portfolio holdings of the Ariel Small Cap Value Concentrated Composite.

A glossary of financial terms provided herein may be found on our website at www.arielinvestments.com.

Index returns reflect the reinvestment of income and other earnings. Indexes are unmanaged, and investors cannot invest directly in an index. The Russell 2000® Value Index measures the performance of the small-cap value segment of the U.S. equity universe. It includes those Russell 2000 companies with lower price-to-book ratios, lower forecast growth and lower sales per share historical growth. Its inception date is June 1, 1993. The Russell 2000® Index measures the performance of the small-cap segment of the U.S. equity universe. The Russell 2000 Index is a subset of the Russell 3000® Index representing approximately 10% of the total market capitalization of that index. It includes approximately 2,000 of the smallest securities based on a combination of their market cap and current index membership. Its inception date is January 1, 1984. data.title: JSML: Cautious Outlook But A Clear Improvement Over Russell 2000 Growth ETFs
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The Janus Henderson Small Cap Growth Alpha ETF (NASDAQ:JSML) has outperformed its benchmark, represented by the iShares Russell 2000 Growth ETF (IWO) since its inception on February 24, 2016. However, current selections show decelerating earnings growth, leading me to believe the fund's proprietary strategy overemphasizes historical results and growth rates instead of expected ones. While I appreciate the fund's dedication to selecting low-risk, high-quality stocks, I'm slightly skeptical of its near-term prospects, so I'm only assigning it a "hold" rating today. I look forward to explaining why and comparing JSML with some alternatives in the article below.

JSML Overview

Strategy Discussion

The Janus Henderson website lists three reasons to invest in JSML, which I've paraphrased below.

1. The primary objective is to identify small-cap companies with the potential for long-term sustainable growth.

2. A proprietary system evaluates small-cap stocks on ten fundamental factors, including sales growth rates, operating profit, earnings per share, and return on invested capital.

3. The system seeks high risk-adjusted returns by avoiding small-cap stocks without solid fundamentals.

Growth, quality, and risk management are at the heart of the proprietary system, but it's crucial to know strong growth is not consistently emphasized. To illustrate, consider how I have ranked JSML on key fundamental factors since my rankings began in July 2024. The sample size is 16.

Notice how JSML consistently ranks well on risk and quality, but its #13/16 average growth rank suggests the factor actually isn't very important in the process. That's probably because the strategy eliminates many ultra-high-growth stocks that tend to be more speculative than stable, contributing to the fund's average #2/16 risk rank.

As for the other factors, JSML's average #5/16 value rank is solid, though its momentum and sentiment ranks are typically poor. The latter indicates its selections are priced cheaply for a good reason (i.e., poor earnings revisions from Wall Street analysts), so I'm inclined to believe the system doesn't track statistics like these that can be important catalysts for growth.

Performance Analysis

Since its inception, the Small Cap Alpha Index has gained an annualized 13.07% compared to 11.42% for the Russell 2000 Growth Index. The fund's 0.30% accounts for most of the fund's tracking error, ultimately reducing returns to 12.74% per year. IWO's expense ratio is 0.24%, so the impact is a little less, but you can also go with the Vanguard Russell 2000 Growth ETF (VTWG), whose expense ratio is just 0.10%.

The next chart compares JSML's total returns since its inception with the Vanguard Small Cap Growth ETF (VBK), the SPDR S&P 600 Small Cap Growth ETF (SLYG), and the iShares Russell 2000 Growth ETF (IWO). As shown, JSML's 188.58% total return was noticeably better than SLYG and IWO and marginally better than VBK.

Still, these returns are impressive, given how small-cap growth stocks have lagged behind their larger counterparts since JSML's inception and that VBK actually holds several mid-cap stocks. To illustrate, VBK's weighted average market cap is about $10.6 billion, compared to $3.4 billion for JSML. VBK's strong returns are likely due to its wider selection universe, and not necessarily a result of a superior strategy.

An analysis on Portfolio Visualizer adds some additional color to JSML's historical risk and return features. Surprisingly, it was the most volatile of the four, evidenced by a 23.18% annualized standard deviation figure. Its 36.16% drawdown from September 2021 to September 2022 was also the highest, but overall, JSML had solid downside risk-adjusted returns, evidenced by a 0.87 Sortino Ratio. This ratio is more useful than the Sharpe Ratio because only downside risk matters to investors. Logically, investors have no issue with volatility resulting in abnormally high returns.

Recall that high risk-adjusted returns were one of the reasons to invest in JSML, so it seems to have accomplished that. However, let's dig deeper into the fund's composition and fundamentals to learn more.

JSML Analysis

Sector Allocations: Overweight Industrials / Health Care

The following table highlights the sector allocation differences between JSML, VBK, SLYG, and IWO. I also included the Pacer US Small Cap Cash Cows Growth Leaders (CAFG) because, despite a relatively poor track record against JSML, it currently offers compelling fundamentals and could make a good satellite holding. JSML and CAFG overlap by just 14.43%.

As shown, JSML overweights Industrials by 9.21% on average, with offsets in Consumer Discretionary, Energy, Real Estate, and Technology. Between Industrials and Health Care, JSML allocates over 50%, so it's a pretty concentrated fund despite holding 202 stocks.

Backtesting JSML: 150 Stocks / 98% By Weight

Before we evaluate JSML's fundamentals, I want to present the results of a three-year backtest of JSML's current holdings and weights, with rebalancings set to quarterly. Using Portfolio Visualizer's backtest tool, I was able to do this for 150/202 stocks, which comprise 98% of the fund. Here are the results.

These results are so good that I have to assume recent performance is a key part of JSML's selection process. This group of stocks actually gained 2.20% in 2022 compared to a 19.64% decline for the ETF and then bounced back with a tremendous 46.78% gain the following year. I haven't come across any ETF that can claim top-level performance in both years, so to say I'm skeptical would be an understatement.

Fundamentals By Top 25 Holdings

With this in mind, please consider the following table that highlights selected fundamental metrics for JSML's top 25 holdings, which comprise 50.81% of the portfolio. Please note this concentration level is substantially higher than VBK, SLYG, and IWO and slightly more than CAFG despite having twice the number of constituents.

Here are three additional observations to consider:

1. Recall how JSML's two standout features are risk management and high quality, which this table nicely demonstrates. First, JSML's 1.11 five-year beta is substantially lower than the broad-based growth funds (VBK, SLYG, and IWO) and supported by high allocations to Health Care stocks like Corcept Therapeutics (CORT) and Catalyst Pharmaceuticals (CPRX). Second, JSML's return on assets, return on equity, and return on total capital figures are excellent, reflecting the manager's preference for capital efficiency. I noticed how only 8/202 holdings (2.67% by weight) have a negative ROTC compared to 396/1060 (28.99% by weight) for IWO, so there's clearly a substantial improvement in quality that, I believe, is responsible for JSML's outperformance.

2. On the other hand, JSML's forward-looking growth statistics aren't as strong as its peers. For example, its 11.85% one-year estimated earnings per share growth rate is 2-6% worse than its peers, but interestingly, its 17.90% three-year historical earnings growth rate is the best of the bunch. This begs the question of which statistic is more relevant, and in my view, it's the former. Small-cap growth stocks, by definition, are immature, and you want to see growth rates accelerate, not decelerate.

DoubleVerify Holdings (DV) is a nice example of the disconnect. Its three-year historical earnings growth rate is 93.29%, but its one-year estimated growth rate is only 23.04%, and investors have reacted negatively with a 45.90% price decline over the last year. I also found that stocks with decelerating earnings growth gained 10.96% over the last year on average compared to 35.74% for those with accelerating earnings growth, so I think estimated rates definitely matter, but it's not something JSML's managers seem to emphasize.

3. The offset to weaker growth rates are more attractive valuation ratios. JSML trades at just 15.79x forward earnings, and if you view it from a growth-at-a-reasonable-price perspective, it's a decent play. When dividing each ETF's forward P/E ratios by their one-year estimated earnings per share growth rates, we get the following:

  • JSML: 15.79x / 11.85% = 1.33x
  • VBK: 25.81x / 16.28% = 1.59x
  • SLYG: 18.45x / 15.12% = 1.22x
  • IWO: 20.59x / 18.26% = 1.13x
  • CAFG: 16.59x / 14.00% = 1.19x

These modified PEG ratios indicate investors should avoid VBK, as it's just too pricey for the growth offered. IWO is attractive but too speculative based on its poor quality metrics, while SLYG offers a nice broad-based compromise. As for JSML and CAFG, they are both high-quality funds, but CAFG looks to have a slight edge at the moment. The challenge is the ETF is too new to know whether these fundamental advantages will continue.

Investment Recommendation

After reviewing JSML's historical results, factor rankings, and fundamentals, I'm stuck between a "buy" and a "hold" rating. I like its consistency in emphasizing low-risk, high-quality stocks trading at attractive valuations. However, I'm discouraged by its constituents' decelerating earnings growth rates, which is an anomaly in the small-cap growth space. I also get the impression that JSML's proprietary strategy overemphasizes recent returns that might not be relevant for the segment. I will err on the side of caution today with a "hold" rating, but for current IWO shareholders, I strongly recommend switching. JSML filters out most of the junk stocks in the Russell 2000 Index, and in the long run, that strategy should be successful.
